transcript.text: Hello!
Hi.
Hi, how are you?
I'm fine.
I was just about to text Bridget if the meeting was still holding.
Oh, yeah.
Oh, yeah.
No, I apologize.
I've been a minute late.
I had one of those where I had to finish sending an invite before I could open this one.
So I apologize for keeping you waiting for a sec.
Well, it is great to meet you.
And let me just give you a maybe a 30-second overview of who I am and why we're talking.
And then we can kind of go into the things I'd love to learn from you.
So I am a consultant and my background is running post-sale success for high-growth companies.
So, for example, I was at DocuSign when they were about 50 million to begin with.
And they worked their global support operations and their first customer success organization and business and insights, et cetera.
Up through taking the
And so I've done that a few times of helping high growth companies set the stage to be successful for high growth.
And so I've been brought in now to help all of you of, you know, you've done zero to 14 million in 10 months as a company, which is incredible.
And the set the stage for going to 50 million is right there for next year and beyond.

And the question I'd like to help you all with is what needs to be in place for you to grow, preferably frictionlessly, or at least smartly, where, you know, your jobs are easier, you feel focused, and there's a lot of momentum.
And so to do that, I am talking to folks like you to hear a couple things.
Like, I'd love a start, if you would.
I know there was just some organizational changes on Monday, but I'd love to hear your perspective.
First of all, how long have you been with GrowthX?
I think it's about eight months or seven.
I joined in April.
Okay.
All right.
So you're actually one of the more experienced folks who's been around.
Okay.
So I would love to hear about your role today, if you would, if you could just walk me through a day in the life of.

And what I'm curious about as you walk me through that is, are there opportunities in your mind that we could invest in as a company, whether it's process, training, tooling, who knows what, that would make it easier for you to do your job?
I'd love to hear some of your observations, if you would.
Exactly.
Interestingly, Bridget asked us not to prepare for this meeting, that we didn't have to prepare.
Honestly, we had the time out of like, maybe like reading some things now.
But I think generally, also the changes that they made on Monday rides, it didn't affect me because I'm still keeping like this.
The clients, right?
Remind me, are you an engagement manager?
No, no, no, content manager.
Content manager, okay, thank you.
So I'm still keeping the same clients, so I don't have to start learning about the client.
But regarding opportunities, I would say, this has crossed my mind a lot of times, and it's really the, I don't know how much you know about the workflow.
So we, Atlas, you know Atlas, right?
Oh, yeah, yeah.

So when they started rolling out the agency pipelines, or before they started rolling them out, we were required to do like some documentation so that they tried to tailor it to like the clients, right?
But when it still came out eventually, we still, I still had to like, you know, kind of find some needs for my clients specifically.
So in that regard, I think one thing that could have made my life easier, because there was a time I had to, I always had to call Panza and I was ranting, like, oh, this is not working for me at all.
But yeah, we had a meeting and he showed me like some things, which to be honest, meetings is easier.
But in my opinion, I think what could have happened then is, if we had, because when we moved from, we used to be on air ops.
Yeah.
Then we moved to like Atlas.
And during the transition state, they gave everyone in the community, I think like a week to get used to like how things were working.
But for the Atlas, for a genetic workflow, we didn't have that.
And I think it's because for air ops, we migrated at once.
But for Atlas, it was like, oh, accounts, the prioritized accounts that were close to renewal, or those that were not really doing well.
So the migration was not at once.
And we kind of didn't have the time to, you know, fine tune it.
So we had to be, I had to be trying to deliver on the articles, at the same time trying to optimize the process.
I mean, some folks were able to like steer through because, again.
Definitely.
So what I think would have happened then was that if we had the time or if, and this might be like resource intensive, I don't think it's like work anyway.
For every engineer that is like, you know, migrating to like changing, creating a workflow, Or an Atlas Pipeline for a client.
Maybe just attach me as a content manager to the engineer so that maybe in two days or a day, right?
We just nearly, maybe not nearly entirely, but we, we get as close to what's done.
So what's currently happening is that they create the workflows or the pipelines, right?
Then they are like, oh, we are done.
You guys should test and let me know what you need or what you need to fix.
I think that would have saved a lot of time.
If originally we just, okay, um, this CM for this account, we are giving you this engineer, we are creating this pipeline for you this week.
Make sure you just sort it out.
So that way.

Again, why this might not work is because, you know, engineers and writers, the technical gap might be, but I have a background in computer science, so I would just say, oh, these things that you're adding, you probably don't need to add them, do them this way, so that when the final thing comes out from the pipeline, the changes I have to make at the end of the day, they are not a lot.

But at the moment, the things that are currently, like, stress me a lot, to generate an article is not, you can generate an article under, like, 15, 20 minutes, and, but it's the post-processing now, that is usually, like, the adding internal links, ensuring that some things that the LLM is telling you they are accurate, so that takes, like, a whole lot of my own time, like, trying to just get it right.
Right.
And now, imagine you have to do that across, like, two clients.
Some, again, some people are adding, like, three clients, and they are, I used to, maybe I'm just the one that's slow, but the post-processing.
Well, I mean, if.
If I may ask, so to confirm, I mean, but you're trying to work in the product where I hear a lot of people to expedite the focus on, oh, I need to get the content out.
They work outside of the product.
But is that one of the reasons I think you're frustrated is you're trying to make all of this work in the product and feedback with engineering and team.
And is that fair?
To be honest, I, yes, recently, yes.
But before, right, before, when the pipelines came out initially, after testing them, I just lost faith.
And I was doing most of the work outside the product.
But after my meeting with Panzer, I saw, like, what could be done with the product itself.
That, oh, if you're actually finding this, this will save you a lot of time.
But now, it's not a mixture of the product and outside of the product.
I use Cloud a lot.
Because, again, at last, is very, the agency pipeline, to be honest, is very good if you just get likes.
Some things, right?
But the problem for me now is finding the time to fine-tune it, I still deliver on articles.
Because the first time I tried to fine-tune it, it was, I spent like a whole weekend.
And I was like, oh, I was really happy the next week.
Oh, this has made my life like easier.
So it's a mixture of the product and working outside the product.
Because working outside the product will, again, it might save time, but it's not sustainable at the end of the day.
If you have to deliver a lot of articles, yeah.
I have a question.
I've been hearing a lot about uneven training or knowledge of product updates and sort of just the level field of who knows what when.
might be you know things because you called Panzer because you have a relationship, so you learned.
But that doesn't mean that the content manager and the other pod knows the same tricks that you
We just learned because they did not call Panzer, for example.
If you got to be king for a day and wave your magic wands, what would you think would be useful in terms of training from the company to the different roles to maximize product usage and efficiency?
To be honest, I think it would be, as a CM, I think, I know, like, some CMs are, like, really, really good.
And every now and then, I go to, like, pick their brains, like, oh, how are you doing this work?
How are doing, like, more articles in, like, less time, and I'm here struggling with it?
So if I were to be king for a day, I would probably create a poll where people vote for, like, the best CMs, so to say.
And have those guys, like, train on best practices that they use.
That's what I would just like to do.
Yeah, okay.
Okay.
Train on best practices.

Anything else like around, anything else that you feel like not even just you, but when you observe around the company you think would be good training or enablement to get everybody from us, you know, to the same baseline of knowledge?
Train enablement.
I think another thing that would work is, I think Panzer and Nana, they are very like good with like massaging the workflows to get to a good point.
If we can have something where they just like evaluate everybody's pipelines or workflows to see what might be missing.
Again, this would be very intensive and it would take a lot of time, but in the long run, I think it's a safe time.
if we can have Panzer and maybe Nana.
And to be honest, we've been having like a genetic meetings, right?
Yeah.
But everybody...
I can't speak for myself.
Those agentic meetings sometimes, we just feel like, oh, this thing can, we see it, it's like, you see something working, but ultimately, don't know how it translates into working for you sometimes.
I want to make sure I heard that right.
So, because I've been hearing a lot of feedback about the agentic meetings.
So could you just restate what you just said again?
So, the agentic meetings, in my opinion, sometimes, we see what's possible.
We like, when they do these meetings, like, oh, this is what you can do, this is what we're currently doing.
But translating that into your own workflow is usually not like the same thing.
Because Panzer has, I don't know, there was a way he showed me, like, one of the workflows, and I was like, this would save me, like, an hour, 13 minutes on, like, the work I'm doing.
But to get mine to that stage, I've tried to copy, again, it would require, like, refining and refining and refining, which we don't have the time for, because...
You also have to deliver on articles, so that's the tricky bit.
Okay.
Okay.
That's super useful.
Anything else in terms of any observations around communications with your pod?
So one of the things that we're looking at, I've been here for about a week, by the way, so I'm still just assimilating what everybody's been telling me.

But one of the things I heard was handoffs between teams, so from like sales to the strategy team to the pods, we're working to better define, you know, what good exit criteria is so that the team has what they need to really understand the customer and be effective in their role.

In your observation, is there anything we should consider when we're talking about handoffs and making sure that across the life cycle of the customer we're sharing information and capturing information about the customer?
I think where I do do that's like really...

Good, because we use Notion, and most of the, I've only had one handoff, right, and the one I had, the person that took over from me, it was really, really helpful, because after the document, it's still set up a meeting for SliceSync.
What's interesting about the work is that, like, everybody can write, but the nuances of each client is just what could drive you crazy.

So, if you don't get it, oh, you say an article, you think it's good, if you write for the clients, you might just have a line that they don't ever want to see, and you don't know, because, like, you don't have enough information.
So, I think for handoff, apart from just, like, documenting, I think there should be, like, a meeting between, like, the person that is handing over and the person that is taking over.

So, again, all this, I think these things happen, but there's just not, like, we, I think the end of last week, people resumed, like, with new clients, this week, I don't think there's enough time to, like, take in all the.
Context that I need to.
Meanwhile, as you're taking over, you're still supposed to deliver a game.
So I think it's just the time.
We already do like a good job of like documenting and handing over everything.
In fact, the documentation is really great.
It's just the time to, oh, this is the information.
I have to estimate this.
I have to know this about these clients.
So yeah, I'm just adding to clients.
Some people have like three.
Yeah.
Experience managers have like eight, which is usually like mind-boggling.
How do you guys manage the context?
Yeah.
So can I repeat back to you like what I think my takeaway is for you to correct?

So I think what I'm hearing, and it's not just from you, there's a lot of change and having a moment just, you know, not, not, we have announced the change and now we're changing and you're trying to do your job and assimilate the change.
The thought is for just ability to create context, pause, best practices.
Simulate.
Just pause.
Not for like weeks, but just a bit more time.
Okay.
So that people can kind of take a breath, get reoriented, and then go faster.
Is that a fair?
Yes.
Okay.
Okay.
Yeah, I've been talking transparently to the team of, you know, with this product launch that we're talking about in February.

I've been kind of hearing this a little bit of, so we're thinking of, can we possibly turn that into an old school GA, like go-to-market, where you have a work-back plan of training and change management and documentation, and everybody has a chance to be level set before it goes to market.
So we'll see if we can figure, figure that out.
So people do have more of a breath before all these new product updates.
Anything else?
Like, you know, I think one of the incredible things about about out.
About this company is that it's global.
Is there anything from an efficiency or a communication perspective that we should consider when we want to make sure people still feel like they have everything they need as we grow?
I can give you an example that I heard from someone else, if useful.
Yeah, you can.
Just give the example.

So the example I heard from someone else was, you know, English isn't everybody's native language per se, and it would be very good to have a marketing lexicon so that all of us are using the same words to mean the same thing, was one example.
And the further point was not everybody comes in with the same level of backgrounds in sort of these terms.
And so, you know, Marcel or Daniel might just start talking, you know, in code language and people might not know what it is.
So there's that
Global element, and then there's just the baseline, you know, vernacular, and then making sure that people were level set in that way was one example that was brought up to me.
I think for me, I'm not sure that's really like a concern, but I think between like teams and within the company, there's like a good understanding.
Where this might apply, this communication thing is with like clients.
There was a time, there was a client recently that, I think I used a particular AI-powered self-service.
That was like, I don't know, it just stuck with me.
And that led to like a series of questions.
She was, she had to ask, oh, am I a native English speaker?
And I was so confused because that was, I could not understand why from that single sentence.
So, maybe for, generally, maybe part of documentation for like clients should be like.
These lexicals and vernaculars I refer to, some clients just have some things that they don't like seeing, they don't want to hear.
Within the company, if I'm texting like a colleague, I would probably not have problems with how they text, so far I understand.
But if it's with the clients, maybe they can have documentation of like, oh, for clients, not for like within the company.
I don't think it's really a problem for me.
Okay, good.

Okay, and so what would need to be true, so let's just pretend the company is doubled in size, and so all the stress, all the stress points are on you to do everything you do today, but twice the size.
What would need to be true for you to be successful?
Oh, everything I'm doing today.
I mean, so if, I think apart from, if we can stick like solely to like writing, and maybe, I'm sorry, research.
home.
a nice 1 OK,
I mean, for you to write a good article, you have to do like your research.
So I think there are some other little administrative tasks that I think they will take like two to three minutes, but sometimes they can take hours.

So there was a time I had to like sort out the, in my former port or with my former manager rights, I think he handled like the, organizing the content OS, doing the SEO research, coming up with the strategy.
All I did back then was, you know, just go to the content OS, pick the topics for the week, write the articles, publish them.
But with my new manager, I think they've changed, she's now an engagement director or something.
She wanted me to grow, which is a good thing.
So I had more like doing more of the strategy stuff, which I did not mind, right?
But now the thing is, if you are, if you were not handling strategy and research before, right?
And you were just handling writing the articles, you have enough time to write your article.

But if you now add, like, strategy and research to it, and you're still supposed to deliver the same number of articles in the description, if the workload were to double, I think one thing that I would want more than anything is, at last, as like a one shot, I plug in all my research, and I'm guessing like, an article that is like 90%, good to go.
So, I think that would be one thing, because again, one thing that I waste time a lot for is just trying to get an article from Atlas, like a very, very good place.
So, yeah.
Okay.
Yeah.
Anything else I should consider, again, knowing that, like, my focus is how do I help this company get to, you know, 50 or 100 million, anything else that I should have in my mind to support you all?
Honestly, I wish I prepared for this meeting, you know, and I would have had, like, this, like, say.
Well, I mean, if you have...
Thoughts afterwards, by all means, I'm in the system, so Slack me, no worries whatsoever, I'm not rolling off in a week, I'm here.
Yeah, yeah, there's problem, I'll just Slack you.
Okay, okay, all right, well, I appreciate your time, and especially, you know, I love as your, I would love more of your feedback, so as you have an observation, let me know.

I'll just share to kind of give you, I've been hearing things about, I hear frustration, which it sounds like you don't have, of people can't find things in Notion, but the people who are making the content feel like it's in Notion.
I'm hearing things about, you know, people feeling like a lot is being communicated about the product, but it's fractured or inconsistent, what people hear or learn.
So we're looking at the, just how do we...
Maybe more formally, not rigidly, but formally structure communication and handoffs and training.
And there's just a couple of like easy, quick wins, we're hoping.

So if you are in a situation and you realize you know something and someone doesn't know something or vice versa, and it's a flag for you about something we should solve systemically, I'd love to hear your observations.
I think one that comes to mind is that, so there are usually updates, like product updates, like, oh, we added this to Atlas, we are adding this next week.
Sometimes those messages, because if I have to check, like, articles I have to write, a message from my director, like, other more pressing clients work, right?
By the end of the day, I'm only scrolling through, like, I'm not fully understanding the product updates.
So I think it would be good if you have, like, maybe a weekly recap or a monthly recap of, oh, this
This is what you guys can't do now.
I mean, they give it out.
It gets to a point in Slack that messages are just messages.
are just free.
Yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah.
So I think if you can have the recaps, like weekly recap or monthly recap of like, oh, these are the new things that you guys should probably try out.
Because the last time I used Atlas, I went there like today or yesterday, and I'm seeing new things, which I'm sure they must have announced on the channel.
Yeah.
But because you have other things, you probably just don't go down to it.
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
Okay, I hear that.
And the other thing I think I heard from you earlier, so a recap of updates in one place so you're not hunting for them.
And then I feel like the other point that you made earlier is, and then tie it to what it means for my job.
Like there's an update, but what does it mean for me and how I do my work?
Is that fair?
Yeah, It doesn't.
Okay.
All right.
Okay.
Well, thank you so much for your time.
And I look forward to hearing from you, you know, as you think a little bit more.
I'm sorry, I should have sent an agenda in advance.
I wasn't sure who knew what.
Anyway, so I'll make sure next time you have something you can see so you feel a little bit more grounded.
All right.
Take care.
Bye-bye.
Yeah, you too.
Bye.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Ella's listening tour to identify operational friction points for scaling.](https://fathom.video/share/hsZseRzPK2d4H4hx_uWu9kgKygpcy-zH?tab=summary&timestamp=25.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [**Atlas Workflow Friction:** The Atlas pipeline migration was rushed, forcing Content Managers (CMs) to optimize workflows while delivering articles, leading to inefficient post-processing (e.g., fact-checking, linking).](https://fathom.video/share/hsZseRzPK2d4H4hx_uWu9kgKygpcy-zH?tab=summary&timestamp=194.0)
  - [**Ineffective Training:** Current training is fractured. "Agentic meetings" show potential but lack actionable steps for CMs, while critical knowledge is gained informally from peers or experts like Panza.](https://fathom.video/share/hsZseRzPK2d4H4hx_uWu9kgKygpcy-zH?tab=summary&timestamp=517.0)
  - [**Unsustainable Workload:** CMs now handle strategy and research on top of writing, without a corresponding reduction in article quotas. This makes scaling impossible without a tool that produces near-final drafts.](https://fathom.video/share/hsZseRzPK2d4H4hx_uWu9kgKygpcy-zH?tab=summary&timestamp=1108.0)
  - [**Communication Overload:** Critical product updates are lost in Slack noise. A weekly/monthly recap is needed to consolidate changes and explain their impact on CM workflows.](https://fathom.video/share/hsZseRzPK2d4H4hx_uWu9kgKygpcy-zH?tab=summary&timestamp=1347.0)

## Topics

### Atlas Workflow Friction

  - [The rushed Atlas pipeline migration (from AirOps) created a major bottleneck.](https://fathom.video/share/hsZseRzPK2d4H4hx_uWu9kgKygpcy-zH?tab=summary&timestamp=245.0)
  - [**Problem:** Engineers build pipelines in isolation, then hand them off to CMs for testing and optimization.](https://fathom.video/share/hsZseRzPK2d4H4hx_uWu9kgKygpcy-zH?tab=summary&timestamp=335.0)
  - [**Impact:** CMs must optimize workflows while simultaneously delivering articles, creating a time conflict.](https://fathom.video/share/hsZseRzPK2d4H4hx_uWu9kgKygpcy-zH?tab=summary&timestamp=288.0)
  - [**Result:** High post-processing overhead (fact-checking, linking) is required to get articles from Atlas to a usable state.](https://fathom.video/share/hsZseRzPK2d4H4hx_uWu9kgKygpcy-zH?tab=summary&timestamp=381.0)
  - [**Proposed Solution:** A 1–2 day, dedicated collaboration session between the CM and engineer during pipeline creation.](https://fathom.video/share/hsZseRzPK2d4H4hx_uWu9kgKygpcy-zH?tab=summary&timestamp=302.0)

### Ineffective Training & Knowledge Sharing

  - [Current training methods fail to create a baseline of knowledge across the team.](https://fathom.video/share/hsZseRzPK2d4H4hx_uWu9kgKygpcy-zH?tab=summary&timestamp=517.0)
  - [**"Agentic Meetings":** Show what's possible but don't provide actionable steps for CMs to implement in their own workflows.](https://fathom.video/share/hsZseRzPK2d4H4hx_uWu9kgKygpcy-zH?tab=summary&timestamp=654.0)
  - [**Informal Knowledge Gaps:** Critical knowledge is gained informally (e.g., Usman learned key workflow optimizations from Panza), creating inconsistent skill levels.](https://fathom.video/share/hsZseRzPK2d4H4hx_uWu9kgKygpcy-zH?tab=summary&timestamp=517.0)
  - [**Proposed Training:**](https://fathom.video/share/hsZseRzPK2d4H4hx_uWu9kgKygpcy-zH?tab=summary&timestamp=543.0)
      - [Peer-led training from top-performing CMs on best practices.](https://fathom.video/share/hsZseRzPK2d4H4hx_uWu9kgKygpcy-zH?tab=summary&timestamp=543.0)
      - [Expert-led (Panza, Nana) review of individual CM pipelines to identify optimization opportunities.](https://fathom.video/share/hsZseRzPK2d4H4hx_uWu9kgKygpcy-zH?tab=summary&timestamp=624.0)

### Unsustainable Workload & Handoffs

  - [The CM role has expanded to include strategy and research, but article quotas remain unchanged.](https://fathom.video/share/hsZseRzPK2d4H4hx_uWu9kgKygpcy-zH?tab=summary&timestamp=1108.0)
  - [**Impact:** This expanded scope makes scaling impossible without a tool that produces near-final drafts.](https://fathom.video/share/hsZseRzPK2d4H4hx_uWu9kgKygpcy-zH?tab=summary&timestamp=1108.0)
  - [**Handoffs:**](https://fathom.video/share/hsZseRzPK2d4H4hx_uWu9kgKygpcy-zH?tab=summary&timestamp=741.0)
      - [Notion documentation is effective.](https://fathom.video/share/hsZseRzPK2d4H4hx_uWu9kgKygpcy-zH?tab=summary&timestamp=777.0)
      - [**Critical Need:** A live meeting between the outgoing and incoming CM to transfer client nuances (e.g., specific phrases to avoid).](https://fathom.video/share/hsZseRzPK2d4H4hx_uWu9kgKygpcy-zH?tab=summary&timestamp=796.0)
      - [**Constraint:** Insufficient time for new CMs to absorb context before delivery deadlines.](https://fathom.video/share/hsZseRzPK2d4H4hx_uWu9kgKygpcy-zH?tab=summary&timestamp=816.0)

### Communication Overload

  - [Critical product updates are lost in the high volume of daily Slack messages.](https://fathom.video/share/hsZseRzPK2d4H4hx_uWu9kgKygpcy-zH?tab=summary&timestamp=1347.0)
  - [**Proposed Solution:** A weekly or monthly recap consolidating updates and explaining their direct impact on CM workflows.](https://fathom.video/share/hsZseRzPK2d4H4hx_uWu9kgKygpcy-zH?tab=summary&timestamp=1347.0)
  - [**Client Communication:** A client-facing lexicon is needed to prevent misunderstandings (e.g., a client questioned Usman's native English after a single phrase).](https://fathom.video/share/hsZseRzPK2d4H4hx_uWu9kgKygpcy-zH?tab=summary&timestamp=1043.0)

## Next Steps

  - [**Ella:**](https://fathom.video/share/hsZseRzPK2d4H4hx_uWu9kgKygpcy-zH?tab=summary&timestamp=917.0)
      - [Explore a formal GTM-style launch plan for the February product release, including structured training and change management.](https://fathom.video/share/hsZseRzPK2d4H4hx_uWu9kgKygpcy-zH?tab=summary&timestamp=917.0)
      - [Investigate creating a weekly/monthly product update recap that explains the impact on CM workflows.](https://fathom.video/share/hsZseRzPK2d4H4hx_uWu9kgKygpcy-zH?tab=summary&timestamp=1347.0)
      - [Consider a client-facing lexicon to standardize communication.](https://fathom.video/share/hsZseRzPK2d4H4hx_uWu9kgKygpcy-zH?tab=summary&timestamp=1043.0)
  - [**Usman:**](https://fathom.video/share/hsZseRzPK2d4H4hx_uWu9kgKygpcy-zH?tab=summary&timestamp=1254.0)
      - [Slack Ella with any further observations or ideas.](https://fathom.video/share/hsZseRzPK2d4H4hx_uWu9kgKygpcy-zH?tab=summary&timestamp=1254.0)

action_items.description: Send agenda to Usman before next meeting
